What are the main purposes of each of these documents used in procurement and supply?
Identify which documents you utilise within your organisation?
Consider the details required to make these documents effective.

Order Acknowledgment
Invitation to Tender
Tender
Contract
An established, widely understood and accepted process
Compliance with policy, process and procedures
The creation of a written document, complimented by supporting information, satisfying defined needs
Documentation used in procurement and supply
Compliance with processes versus the achievement of outcomes
Regulatory requirements that can impact on procedures
Corporate governance applying to procurement and supply
The use of ethical codes in procurement and supply
Driving Value through Procurement and Supply
In this Unit, we will focus on:
A simple representation of the process is:
Organisations typically have procedures and policies that regulate the process

Source the market
Identify potential suppliers

Invite quotations or tenders
Request for quotation (RFQ) or invitation to tender
Analyse quotations & select most promising supplier

Contract/supplier management
Monitor, review & maintain performance

Identify the need
Requisition or bill of materials

Ensures that all tasks are performed that need to be performed
Maintains adequate co-ordination of effort between stakeholders
Promotes consistency in processes and outcomes
Prevents conflict and sub-optimal behaviour
Supports good governance and managerial control
Compliance with relevant standards, law and regulation
Supports the systematic development of procurement staff, systems, technology and other resources
It fosters efficiency

It enables the documentation and sharing of good practice
It enables meaningful process analysis, problem-solving and improvement
It supports the devolution of some procurement tasks to non-procurement staff

The term separation of duties means that the processes involved in procurement and supply management are divided between two or more people so that no one person is responsible for the entire purchasing process.
It essentially involves three key authorities:
Authority to seek quotations, negotiate and commit
Authorisations or sign-offs may be required:
When a requisition is originated in a user department
Before a specification is released to a supplier
When the purchase order is completed
When the supplier’s invoice is received
Contract changes must be authorised by the relevant contract manager
Organisations will generally cover requirements for competition - An example…

£0 - £10,000
Seek minimum of  2 Quotations
Where it is not considered advantageous, administratively efficient or practical to seek comparative quotations for purchases below £2,500, one quotation may be sought

£10,000 - £50,000
Seek minimum of 4 Quotations
All requirements must be openly advertised with the process managed using the E-Tendering system unless approval of the procurement team is obtained to run a closed  i.e. non advertised process

£50,000 – £100,000
Formal Request For Quotation Process seeking 4 Quotations
A minimum of 4 suppliers must be invited to submit quotations using an approved format Request for Quotation document.  Optionally a formal tender procedure may be used under £100,000. A formal tendering process must be carried out for all procurements above £100,000 in line with existing procedures

E-Tendering
The management of quotations and tenders using the E-Tendering system is required for all procurements with a value above £10,000
